STATEMENT OF NON OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF REBECCA CHAMORRO'S EX PARTE APPLICATION FOR AN ORDER SHORTENING TIME ON MOTION FOR PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION (TRANSACTION ID # 58399634) FILED BY DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH (INCLUDING DBA MERCY MEDICAL CENTER REDDING)

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION - THE COURT DENIES THE REQUEST FOR A PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION. FIRST, PLAINTIFF IS UNLIKELY TO PREVAIL ON THE MERITS. THE DISCRIMINATION CLAIMS BASED UPON CIVIL CODE SEC. 51 AND GOV'T CODE SEC. 11135 ARE LIKELY TO FAIL BECAUSE DEFENDANT'S STERILIZATION POLICY APPLIES EQUALLY TO MEN AND WOMEN. THE ETHICAL AND RELIGIOUS DIRECTIVE 53 PROVIDES THAT: DIRECT STERILIZATION OF EITHER MEN OR WOMEN, WHETHER PERMANENT OR TEMPORARY, IS NOT PERMITTED IN A CATHOLIC HEALTH CARE INSTITUTION. PROCEDURES THAT INDUCE STERILITY ARE PERMITTED WHEN THEIR DIRECT EFFECT IS THE CURE OR ALLEVIATION OF A PRESENT AND SERIOUS PATHOLOGY AND A SIMPLER TREATMENT IS NOT AVAILABLE. DEFENDANT CORRECTLY NOTES THAT THE CITED STATUTES REGARDING THE CORPORATE PRACTICE OF MEDICINE AND CONTRACEPTION DO NOT EXPRESSLY PROVIDE FOR A PRIVATE RIGHT OF ACTION. IN ADDITION, DEFENDANT SHOWS THAT IT DID NOT VIOLATE HEALTH & SAFETY CODE SEC. 1258 BECAUSE IT DOES NOT PERMIT "STERILIZATION OPERATIONS FOR CONTRACEPTIVE PURPOSES'" PLAINTIFF PRESENTS EVIDENCE SHOWING THAT THE ONLY PURPOSE OF A TUBAL LIGATION IS FOR CONTRACEPTIVE PURPOSES. PLAINTIFF FURTHER ARGUES THAT DEFENDANT HAS ALLOWED THE PROCEDURE ON NUMEROUS OCCASIONS. THERE IS INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE IN THE RECORD, HOWEVER, TO DEMONSTRATE THAT THE PRIOR TUBAL LIGATION PROCEDURES WERE SOLELY FOR CONTRACEPTIVE PURPOSES AS OPPOSED TO CURING OR ALLEVIATING A PRESENT AND SERIOUS PATHOLOGY AND A SIMPLER TREATMENT WAS NOT AVAILABLE IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE RELIGIOUS DIRECTIVES. (DR. VAN KIRK DEC., PAR. 15; DR. DE SOTO DEC., PAR. 8-10). PLAINTIFF ALSO FAILS TO SHOW THAT THE BALANCE OF EQUITIES AND HARMS WEIGHS IN HER FAVOR. PLAINTIFF CAN OBTAIN THE DESIRED PROCEDURE AT OTHER HOSPITALS THAT DO NOT FOLLOW DEFENDANT'S DIRECTIVES. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: ERNEST GOLDSMITH. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. MOTION / Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel
Off Calendar Feb-17-2017 Text Ruling

Defendant Dignity Health Dba Mercy Medical Center Redding Notice Of Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Feb-09-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Christine Haskett, of Covington & Burling LLP, Tel. 415.591.7087, for plaintiffs;
Elizabeth Gill, of American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California, Tel. 415.621.2493, for plaintiffs;
Barry S. Landsberg, of Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP, Tel. 310.312.4259, for defendant Dignity Health;
Harvey L. Rochman, of Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP, Tel. 310.312.4104, for defendant Dignity Health.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT DIGNITY HEALTH Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings is GRANTED with twenty days leave to amend to allow plaintiffs the opportunity to allege a writ of mandate claim based per CCP 1085 in lieu of their UCL claim. Dignity's motion does not run afoul of CCP 438(g)(1). A common law motion for judgment on the pleadings may be filed after a demurrer without the need to show a material change in the law. Moreover, Dignity's motion is permissible because it amplifies issues that were not flushed out by the demurrer. All of Dignity's substantive arguments are rejected except its argument that the proper procedural vehicle for plaintiffs' claim is a writ of mandate. Health and Safety Code 1258 can apply to Dignity because it allegedly performs tubal ligations. Paragraphs 23, 53-61, and 85-86 of the first amended complaint sufficiently allege a violation of section 1258. Dignity's argument that its decision to allow or disallow tubal ligations based on "advanced maternal age" and "grand multiparity" necessarily involves medical criteria that do not implicate section 1258 is not amenable to resolution on a demurrer. Dignity raises a factual issue that can only be decided on a factual record. Section 1258 expressly states that age and number of children can be nonmedical qualifications and plaintiffs allege that maternal age and grand multiparity are factors that Dignity considers when deciding whether to permit postpartum tubal ligations. (FAC 59.) In addition, the first amended complaint alleges that plaintiffs have not been provided with a clear set of criteria when Dignity allows postpartum tubal ligations. (FAC 59-61.) The demurrer order determined that plaintiffs have standing and have suffered injuries as a result of the complained of actions by Dignity. There is no basis to abstain. This court is capable of making a determination whether Dignity violated section 1258, an issue which does not involve the type of technical or specialized knowledge and experience or consideration of complex economic policies which warrants abstention. Because plaintiffs are challenging the legality of Dignity's quasi-legislative policies, case law establishes that plaintiffs' remaining claim should be styled as one for writ of mandate. The court expresses no view whether the label of writ of mandate carries any substantive differences in matters of proof or evidence. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Felicia Green; Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org; Dept. 302.

Plaintiff'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction
Off Calendar Jan-14-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Elizabeth Gill of American Civil Liberties Union telephone number 415-621-2493 for Plaintiff.
Christine Haskett of Covington & Burling LLP telephone number 415-591-7087 for Plaintiff.
Harvey Rochman of Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP telephone number 310-312-4104 for Defendant.
Barry Landsberg of Manatt, Phelps & Phillips, LLP telephone number 310-312-4259 for Defendant.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling. Plaintiff's Motion For Preliminary Injunction - The court denies the request for a preliminary injunction. First, plaintiff is unlikely to prevail on the merits. The discrimination claims based upon Civil Code sec. 51 and Gov't Code sec. 11135 are likely to fail because defendant's sterilization policy applies equally to men and women. The Ethical and Religious Directive 53 provides that: Direct sterilization of either men or women, whether permanent or temporary, is not permitted in a Catholic health care institution. Procedures that induce sterility are permitted when their direct effect is the cure or alleviation of a present and serious pathology and a simpler treatment is not available.
Defendant correctly notes that the cited statutes regarding the corporate practice of medicine and contraception do not expressly provide for a private right of action. In addition, defendant shows that it did not violate Health & Safety Code sec. 1258 because it does not permit "sterilization operations for contraceptive purposes'" Plaintiff presents evidence showing that the only purpose of a tubal ligation is for contraceptive purposes. Plaintiff further argues that defendant has allowed the procedure on numerous occasions. There is insufficient evidence in the record, however, to demonstrate that the prior tubal ligation procedures were solely for contraceptive purposes as opposed to curing or alleviating a present and serious pathology and a simpler treatment was not available in accordance with the religious directives. (Dr. Van Kirk Dec., par. 15; Dr. De Soto Dec., par. 8-10). Plaintiff also fails to show that the balance of equities and harms weighs in her favor. Plaintiff can obtain the desired procedure at other hospitals that do not follow defendant's directives. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/EG)
